The Berry That Turns Lemons Into LemonadeWhat Is Miracle Fruit? The Berry That Turns Lemons Into LemonadeColin GomezEat, Drink5 months agoShare It looks like a common hedge-row berry and tastes of very little at all. Yet, within this modest West African fruit lies a chemical quirk capable of turning the sensory world upside down. Today, we’re casting the spotlight on miracle fruit. Its name itself is enough to stir interest; then throw in its exotic origins from the understorey of the forests of West Africa, and all of a sudden your expectations really start to bubble. But for all its apparent mystique on the face of it, it really is quite an unremarkable-looking berry. And it sure doesn’t taste like much, too, so what gives? Well, the real magic is what happens after you eat the fruit. You see, inside the fruit hides a compound called miraculin, a glycoprotein that binds to the sweetness receptors on the tongue. It and of itself isn’t sweet, but what it does is something quite extraordinary. As soon as something enters a mouth bathed in miraculin, the compound changes shape, activating the receptors and sending a “sweet” signal to the brain. What then happens is a temporary rewiring of your taste buds that turns your sense of taste upside down, as you know it. Photography: MiracleFruitFarm / Wikimedia Commons Take the sourest things you can imagine—lemons, vinegar, and the like—the kinds of things that have you scrunching your face up just thinking about it. Put those in your mouth after chewing on some miracle fruit, and you’ll find, to your surprise, these things that once seemed so sharply acidic have become bewilderingly sweet, sometimes to the point of becoming sickly so. It’s a neat trick that lasts anywhere between half an hour to two hours. This astounding effect was first stumbled upon by West Africans centuries ago, who you imagine would’ve been very much confused when they first discovered it. It was not long before they started putting it to more practical uses at the time, eating the berries to make things like sour palm wine and fermented breads more palatable. It would take until the 18th century before European explorers came across it for the first time, and then until the 1960s before scientists managed to isolate the miraculin itself. Attempts to commercialise it as a low-calorie sweetener followed in the decade after, but regulatory hurdles, in the United States especially, put an end to its potential in that regard. Photography: Creative Commons CC0 / Pxhere Today, miracle fruit occupies aspirations far less lofty. Largely relegated to the realms of obscurity, it now inhabits a specific dimension between those interested in its culinary applications and those who study the science behind it. Food enthusiasts keen to explore the ways in which it bends the notions of taste host “flavour tripping parties”, while some researchers are looking beyond the novelty and exploring miraculin’s potential to alleviate taste disorders. For most of us, though, its charm is rather more straightforward: we just like how it makes things taste like they’re not supposed to. It’s also a reminder that taste, which we already know to be subjective, could be even more speculative than we thought. It’s a pliable, suggestible sensation, and the wider we open our minds to treating it as much, the closer we can get to discovering avenues beyond our wildest expectations.
